normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.requirement_title: The app is transparent about data collection and usage.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-PRIVACY-3 (Description): Users have the right to know how their data is being used. This control ensures that apps provide clear information about data collection, storage, and sharing practices, including any behavior a user wouldn't reasonably expect, such as background data collection. Apps should also adhere to platform guidelines on data declarations.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must provide clear, accurate, accessible information about how user data is collected, stored, used, and shared — including behaviors the user would not reasonably expect (background data collection, third-party sharing not obviously implied by the app's primary function). Transparency surfaces include in-app privacy policies, platform-required disclosures (Apple Privacy Nutrition Labels via App Store Connect, Google Data Safety section via Play Console, PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest on iOS 17+), AI/ML disclosure where applicable, and just-in-time notices for surprising data uses. Disclosures must match actual app behavior; platform store rejection or regulatory liability follows from mismatches.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note:
Applies at Tier 2+ as a conservative corpus-level gate. Per Constitution Pillars 2 and 7, transparency activates at Tier 2+ where the app handles content distribution and analytics; the three mapped traces are SEC-PILLAR-2-EXTERNAL-PROCESSOR-DECLARATION (Tier 2-5), SEC-PILLAR-2-AI-TRANSPARENCY (Tier 2-5, conditional on AI processor presence), and SEC-PILLAR-7-LAST-MILE-COMPLIANCE (Tier 1-5, includes Privacy Nutrition Label / Data Safety section). The blueprint schema declares external_processor_retention[] (blueprint.schema.json:225), ai_transparency (blueprint.schema.json:314), and last_mile_gates (blueprint.schema.json:260) per Constitution v0.5; this requirement file documents the MASVS-PRIVACY-3 mapping against those existing fields. If a Tier 2+ app collects no user data whatsoever (e.g., a fully-anonymous read-only utility), auditors may mark the requirement not-applicable during evidence review with documented justification. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 2+ requires transparency disclosures because Apple App Privacy Details and Google Play Data Safety declarations are platform-store submission surfaces whenever the app collects, uses, shares, or links user data.

**Phase 5 migration (2026-05-21):** Applicability condition migrated to a direct-field check on the per-surface evidence array that backs this control. The new condition asks whether the app actually declares the surface this control governs, replacing the prior tier-or-auth-flow proxy. tier_floor stays unchanged as the universal-floor backstop. References Phase 1 schema addition (commit `9ad9b6c`), Phase 4 applicability evaluator (commit `37ce03d`), and Phase 4.5 evaluator `==` operator extension (Phase 5 commit).

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution covers MASVS-PRIVACY-3 via three supporting traces with no single dedicated primary — SEC-PILLAR-2-EXTERNAL-PROCESSOR-DECLARATION (supporting, provides the data-flow inventory that transparency disclosures consume), SEC-PILLAR-2-AI-TRANSPARENCY (supporting, focused subset for AI-routed data with opt-out + PII pruning + store disclosure), and SEC-PILLAR-7-LAST-MILE-COMPLIANCE (supporting, declares structured input for Apple Privacy Nutrition Labels and Google Data Safety section). Like MASVS-CODE-3, this is a distributed-coverage pattern: no single Constitution trace is dedicated 1:1 to PRIVACY-3 because transparency spans three layers (data-flow inventory, AI-specific disclosure, platform-store disclosure surfaces), and the three traces together substantively cover the scope. The mapping is partial because the three Pillar 2 + Pillar 7 traces address transparency by providing the structured inputs that user-facing disclosures consume; the actual disclosure-text quality, accuracy alignment between declared and actual behavior, and surprising-behavior just-in-time notification flows remain auditor-evaluation surface. Implementation correctness for disclosure-vs-actual-behavior alignment (Apple's App Privacy Details / Google's Data Safety section matching the app's real data flows), AI disclosure prominence, and just-in-time consent ordering requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration.

expected_evidence_patterns: iOS: App Store Connect's App Privacy section configured with declarations matching security_manifest.data_retention_policies[] and security_manifest.external_processor_retention[] — every collected data type, purpose, and linkage-to-identity flag declared

iOS 17+: PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest declares NSPrivacyTracking, NSPrivacyTrackingDomains, NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypes (with corresponding purposes), and NSPrivacyAccessedAPITypes (required-reason API declarations) for all categories the app uses

Android: Play Console's Data safety section configured with declarations matching security_manifest's data flows — data types, purposes, sharing-with-third-parties, encryption-in-transit, deletion-mechanism details
In-app privacy policy reachable from a clearly-labeled link in app settings; policy text matches actual app behavior (not just legal-templated boilerplate); link target is HTTPS

AI-bearing apps disclose AI use prominently — security_manifest.ai_transparency declares the AI processor, what data is routed, opt-out path, store disclosure synchronized; in-app surface explains the routing in user-meaningful language at the point of use
Background data collection disclosed with reasoning — "this app collects location in background to deliver geofenced alerts" rather than silent background access without disclosure

Just-in-time consent surfaces for surprising data uses — accessing contacts triggers an explanatory pre-permission dialog before the system permission prompt; not just relying on iOS Info.plist usage description text alone
last_mile_gates (security_manifest.last_mile_gates) declares: app_store_privacy_details_status, play_console_data_safety_status, privacy_policy_url, in_app_privacy_link_path, ai_disclosure_surfaces[]
failure_indicators: App Store Connect / Play Console privacy declarations DON'T match actual app data flows — declared no data collection but the app actually uses analytics SDKs; declared as 'data not linked to identity' but the app sends identifiable data to processors; app submission rejection risk plus regulatory exposure
Privacy policy text is generic legal-boilerplate not reflecting actual data flows — fails the 'clear information' bar of PRIVACY-3 even when present
Privacy policy link broken, redirects to unrelated content, or hidden behind multiple settings layers — undermines the user's right to know
iOS 17+: PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest missing or stub — Apple submission rejection PLUS PRIVACY-3 violation; mandatory for required-reason APIs
iOS 17+: PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy declares NSPrivacyTracking false while app actually performs cross-app tracking — disclosure-vs-actual mismatch
AI processor used (text routed to OpenAI / Anthropic / etc) without in-app disclosure — user wouldn't reasonably expect AI routing of their text without being told
Background data collection (location, microphone, sensors) without in-app explanation of why — the explicit 'wouldn't reasonably expect' case PRIVACY-3 calls out
Third-party SDK data collection that isn't reflected in the app's declared data flows — undeclared transitive collection
Privacy policy is up-to-date in app store but in-app linked-policy is stale (different version) — version drift undermines transparency
false_positive_traps: Transparency disclosure handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test environments may use stub privacy policies; these are not production violations.

Apps that legitimately collect data the user would obviously expect (e.g., a calendar app collecting calendar events, a fitness app collecting step counts) are not required to use just-in-time pre-permission dialogs for those obvious-purpose flows — the violation surface is surprising data uses without disclosure, not all data uses.

Privacy policy hosted on the company's main website (rather than a dedicated app-specific URL) is NOT a violation as long as the policy section addressing the app's data flows is clearly identifiable and the link resolves to that section.

Apps that legitimately can't disclose specific processor names due to commercial NDA (rare but happens with enterprise integrations) are NOT violations if security_manifest.external_processor_retention declares the data-class and purpose with a sanitized processor description ('enterprise customer's analytics processor') and the actual contract terms align with the disclosure level — full transparency where possible, contract-scoped transparency where required.
iOS apps targeting iOS versions below 17 are NOT subject to PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy requirements — the violation surface applies to apps targeting iOS 17+ where the manifest is mandatory.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 2 external-processor-declaration discipline (SEC-PILLAR-2-EXTERNAL-PROCESSOR-DECLARATION) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-PRIVACY-3 coverage on the data-flow-inventory vector
uses Constitution Pillar 2 AI-transparency discipline (SEC-PILLAR-2-AI-TRANSPARENCY) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-PRIVACY-3 coverage on the AI-routed-data disclosure vector

uses Constitution Pillar 7 last-mile-compliance discipline (SEC-PILLAR-7-LAST-MILE-COMPLIANCE) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-PRIVACY-3 coverage on the platform-store-disclosure vector (Apple Privacy Nutrition Labels, Google Data Safety section)

declares transparency posture in security_manifest.external_processor_retention, security_manifest.ai_transparency, and security_manifest.last_mile_gates per MASVS-PRIVACY-3 platform-best-practice requirement
synchronizes Apple App Privacy Details / Google Data Safety section content with actual app data flows
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-PRIVACY-3. MASVS-PRIVACY-3 is mapped to three Constitution v0.5 traces in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-2-EXTERNAL-PROCESSOR-DECLARATION (supporting, data-flow inventory layer), SEC-PILLAR-2-AI-TRANSPARENCY (supporting, AI-specific disclosure layer), and SEC-PILLAR-7-LAST-MILE-COMPLIANCE (supporting, platform-store-disclosure layer). All three labeled supporting because no single Constitution trace is dedicated 1:1 to PRIVACY-3 — transparency spans three substantively distinct layers and the three traces together cover the scope. The partial-mapping label is honest about this. Claims about transparency are scoped to the structured inputs (declared data flows, AI processor declarations, last-mile-gate configuration) and to the platform-store surfaces those inputs populate; claims do NOT extend to legal-compliance assertions under GDPR / CCPA (MASVS-PRIVACY disclaims substitution for these per the standard's IMPORTANT DISCLAIMER on page 33), to disclosure-text-quality judgments (the auditor evaluates whether disclosure text is user-meaningful, not the requirement file), or to in-the-wild detection of surprising data uses the user would not reasonably expect (auditor-evaluation surface). Out of scope: privacy policy text drafting, regulatory legal review, marketing-claims oversight (these are downstream of the structured inputs PRIVACY-3 covers). Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
